subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Graph of f' — FTC Values, Increasing Intervals, Abs Min & Second Derivative Existence
stimulus: The function $f$ is differentiable on the closed interval $[-6, 5]$ and satisfies $f(-2) = 7$. The graph of $f'$, the derivative of $f$, consists of a semicircle and three line segments, as shown.

rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Uses FTC with initial condition: $f(-6) = f(-2) + \int_{-2}^{-6} f'(x)\, dx = 7 - \int_{-6}^{-2} f'(x)\, dx$
1 pt: Correct value $f(-6)$: from graph geometry $\int_{-6}^{-2} f'(x)\, dx = 4$ (e.g., area of triangle); $f(-6) = 7 - 4 = 3$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $f(-6) = f(-2) - \int_{-6}^{-2} f'(x)\, dx = 7 - 4 = 3$. $f(5) = f(-2) + \int_{-2}^{5} f'(x)\, dx = 7 + (-2\pi + 3) = 10 - 2\pi$. The integral $\int_{-2}^{5} f'(x)\, dx = -2\pi + 3$ from the graph (semicircle area $= -2\pi$ plus triangle/trapezoid area).

parts.prompt: On what intervals is $f$ increasing? Justify your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Justification: $f$ is increasing where $f'(x) > 0$; from the graph, $f'(x) > 0$ on $(-6, -2)$ and $(2, 5)$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $f'(x) > 0$ on $(-6, -2)$ and $(2, 5)$, so $f$ is increasing on $[-6, -2]$ and $[2, 5]$.

parts.prompt: Find the absolute minimum value of $f$ on the closed interval $[-6, 5]$. Justify your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Considers candidates: critical points where $f'(x) = 0$ ($x = -2$ and $x = 2$) and endpoints $x = -6$ and $x = 5$
1 pt: Correct answer with justification: evaluates $f$ at all candidates; $f(-6) = 3$, $f(-2) = 7$, $f(2) = 7 - 2\pi$, $f(5) = 10 - 2\pi$; absolute minimum value is $f(2) = 7 - 2\pi$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $f(2) = f(-2) + \int_{-2}^{2} f'(x)\, dx = 7 + (-2\pi) = 7 - 2\pi$ (semicircle with diameter 4 centered at origin, area $= -2\pi$). Values: $f(-6)=3$, $f(-2)=7$, $f(2)=7-2\pi \approx 0.717$, $f(5)=10-2\pi \approx 3.717$. Absolute minimum value is $7 - 2\pi$ at $x = 2$.

parts.prompt: For each of $f''(-5)$ and $f''(3)$, find the value or explain why it does not exist.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: $f''(-5) = -\frac{1}{2}$ (slope of the linear segment of $f'$ on $(-6, -2)$ is computed from the graph)

1 pt: $f''(3)$ does not exist because the one-sided limits of $\frac{f'(x) - f'(3)}{x - 3}$ are not equal: $\lim_{x \to 3^-} \frac{f'(x) - f'(3)}{x-3} = 2 \ne -1 = \lim_{x \to 3^+} \frac{f'(x) - f'(3)}{x-3}$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $f''(-5) = \frac{2-0}{-2-(-6)} = \frac{2}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$... scoring key says $\frac{2-0}{-2-(-6)} = \frac{1}{2}$... actually $f''(-5) = -\frac{1}{2}$ from slope of $f'$ on $(-6,-2)$ = $\frac{0-2}{-2-(-6)} = \frac{-2}{4} = -\frac{1}{2}$. For $f''(3)$: left-hand limit is $2$, right-hand limit is $-1$; they differ, so $f''(3)$ does not exist.
